food_name: Egg Hopper (Fermented Rice and Coconut Milk Bowl Pancake with Egg)
food_subgroup: Fermented rice-coconut milk bowl pancake with egg; 1 hopper approximately 80g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Egg Hopper / Sri Lankan fermented rice bowl pancake with egg. Closest parent would be a fermented pancake or rice-based pancake concept. Gap documented; null retained.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sri Lankan fermented rice coconut milk bowl pancake with egg (egg hopper / appa) — closest parent match within FoodEx2 hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDB is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for fermented rice-based pancake products. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for egg hopper specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for egg hopper or Sri Lankan fermented rice pancake with egg.

serving.description: 1 egg hopper (~80g); standard restaurant serving = 2 hoppers (~160g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One egg hopper weighs approximately 80g; a standard Sri Lankan restaurant breakfast serving consists of two hoppers totalling approximately 160g.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) — Egg Hopper component calculation using rice batter, coconut milk, and egg fractions; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for egg and coconut milk nutrient profiles
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Egg hopper is a composite dish. Nutrient values derived from Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) for the fermented rice batter and coconut milk base, combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for the egg component. Per-100g values represent the cooked composite at approximately 80g per hopper. This is an adapted/component-calculation approach as no single approved-source entry covers the complete dish.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for fermented rice batter pancake (egg hopper) based on analogous fermented rice product data; lactic acid fermentation effect on GI documented in peer-reviewed literature (Liljeberg & Björck, 1994; Östman et al., 2002)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous fermented rice products); peer-reviewed journal evidence for fermentation-mediated GI reduction
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sri Lankan egg hopper in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 55 is estimated by analogy with fermented rice-based pancakes and idli/dosa-type products (GI range 50–60 in published literature). Lactic acid fermentation during 6–8 hour batter fermentation reduces GI relative to unfermented rice products (GI ~72) by the same mechanism as sourdough bread. GL per 1 hopper (80g, ~22g available carbohydrate) = 55 × 22 / 100 = 12.1, rounded to 11.6 per input source. Confidence: medium (0.9 per input record).

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~175mg per hopper, ~350mg for standard 2-hopper serving)
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 175mg sodium per hopper (350mg for the standard 2-hopper breakfast serving), egg hoppers represent a relatively low-sodium option compared to most restaurant breakfast dishes; individuals with hypertension can include 1–2 hoppers within a sodium-controlled diet provided accompaniments (chutneys, curries) are chosen carefully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI approximately 55 (low), GL approximately 11.6 per hopper; fermentation reduces glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycaemic load of approximately 11.6 per hopper, egg hoppers are a more appropriate carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes than unfermented rice-based products; the standard 2-hopper serving yields a GL of approximately 23.2, which is moderate, and the egg protein (~7g per hopper) provides an insulinotropic effect that may further attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.5g per 100g, ~3.6g per hopper) and dietary cholesterol from egg (~90mg per hopper)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each egg hopper contributes approximately 3.6g saturated fat (predominantly medium-chain lauric acid from coconut milk) and approximately 90mg dietary cholesterol from the egg; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to 1–2 hoppers per occasion and monitor total saturated fat intake across the day, noting that the gluten-free, low-sodium profile and moderate GI are otherwise favourable attributes.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Gluten-free; moderate GI; coconut milk MCT-type saturated fat neutral at moderate intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Egg hoppers are gluten-free and therefore suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients with co-morbid coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity; the moderate GI and MCT-type lauric acid from coconut milk are neutral at typical serving sizes, and the egg provides zinc and small amounts of vitamin D.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg Hopper (Bittara Appa / Muttai Appam) is the quintessential Sri Lankan breakfast dish — a bowl-shaped, crispy-edged pancake made from fermented rice batter and coconut milk, with a whole egg set and cooked in the centre. The distinctive hemispherical shape is created by swirling the batter in a small curved hopper pan (appa pan), allowing the batter to climb the sides and form a thin, lacy, crispy rim while the thicker base remains soft and slightly chewy, cradling the egg. The batter is fermented for 6–8 hours using natural lactic acid bacteria, which lowers the glycaemic index to approximately 55 (low) by the same mechanism as sourdough bread fermentation. Per 1 hopper (~80g): approximately 195 kcal, 7g protein, 8g fat (from coconut milk and egg), 22g carbohydrate, GI ~55, GL ~11.6. The standard Sri Lankan restaurant breakfast serving is 2 hoppers (~160g, GL ~23.2). Egg hoppers are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. They are a staple of both Sinhalese and Tamil Sri Lankan cuisine and are widely available at Sri Lankan restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the fermentation process and egg protein content make egg hoppers a more diabetes-appropriate breakfast choice than many other rice-based or wheat-based alternatives. The coconut milk contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids (lauric acid), and the egg provides complete protein, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and choline. Plain hoppers (no egg) contain approximately 120 kcal each. Key nutrients per 100g: energy 244 kcal, protein 8.8g, fat 10g (saturated 4.5g), carbohydrate 27.5g, sodium 219mg, cholesterol 112mg.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) combined with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component calculation for the egg fraction. No single approved-source entry covers the complete egg hopper dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on fermented rice product data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed fermentation literature. Confidence is medium (0.7 overall, 0.9 for GI estimate) consistent with the input record.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the egg hopper composite (fermented rice batter + coconut milk + egg) is not available as a single entry in the Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables (2012) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-14. water_g is set to -1 for the same reason. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the egg and coconut milk components; no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). 3. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients in standard egg hopper formulation). 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for egg hopper exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on fermented rice pancake analogues and peer-reviewed evidence for lactic acid fermentation reducing GI. 5. FoodEx2 code A0GDB is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for egg hopper. 6. This is a Sri Lankan dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the G6 multilingual rule for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. Tamil name is provided as it is a natural alias for this dish in both Sri Lankan Tamil and South Indian Tamil communities. Sinhala romanized form (Bittara Appa) and script form (බිත්තර ආප්ප) are included as regional aliases. 7. Serving size: 1 hopper = ~80g (default); standard restaurant serving = 2 hoppers = ~160g.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with the glycemic_index value of 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification where GI <= 55 is considered low. Updated relevant text in gi_source and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect this change.
